nativeRange.habitat: Shallow tropical reefs and rocky shoreline crevices, generally from the intertidal zone to about 12 m depth.
nativeRange.biotope: Coral reef and rocky reef hideouts with dense crevice structure.

diet.notes: Generally accepts large frozen meaty foods such as silversides, shrimp, squid, and pieces of fish. Wild diet is dominated by crustaceans (especially crabs) and cephalopods; offering occasional live or shell-on prey tends to be appreciated. New arrivals sometimes need a few days to start feeding and have been reported to respond best to scent-based, targeted feeding with tongs.

goodWith: other robust fish too large to swallow

imageCandidates.notes: Atsme (Betty Wills); 3370x4200. Tack-sharp head close-up showing the diagnostic bold yellow chain-link mosaic on a dark body against a black background; most striking and highest-res of the pool.
Kris Mikael Krister; 2048x1152. Sharp, vibrant in-water head close-up of a different individual, clear yellow-on-dark chain pattern and blunt snout.

Dustin Minialoff (via iNaturalist); 1536x2048. Whole-body tide-pool shot showing the chain-link pattern running the full length of the eel; adds variety vs head-only shots, colours slightly muted through the water surface.

summary: A boldly patterned Atlantic moray with a yellow-on-dark chain-link mosaic. Generally considered one of the more manageable morays, but still a predatory eel requiring a tight-fitting lid and tankmates too large to swallow.
careNotes: The Chain Moray (Echidna catenata) circulates in the trade under several names — Chain Eel, Chainlink Moray, and Chainlink Eel all refer to the same species. Generally regarded as one of the easier moray eels for an intermediate marine keeper, it is still a long-lived predator that needs a large, well-sealed tank with dense rockwork. Typical captive adults reach 40–75 cm; a rare maximum of around 165 cm has been recorded in the wild, but home-aquarium specimens are almost always well below that. A tight-fitting lid with no gaps is essential, as this species carries a high escape risk common to morays generally. Generally considered reef-safe with caution — sessile corals are typically left alone, but ornamental shrimp, small crabs, and other clean-up-crew invertebrates will be eaten. Wild diet leans heavily on crustaceans (crabs in particular) and cephalopods; in captivity it adapts to large frozen meaty foods such as silversides, shrimp, squid, and fish chunks, offered two or three times per week. Tends to be more active at dusk and after lights-out, though individual specimens have been reported foraging during the day as well.
breedingNotes: Not known to have been bred in home aquariums. Like other moray eels, Echidna catenata is presumed to spawn pelagically and pass through a long leptocephalus larval stage that has not been successfully reared in captivity.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care-sheet level info: minimum tank size (125 US gal), temperament, reef-safe-with-caution rating, temperature 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.020-1.025, carnivore diet with live/frozen meaty foods.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Muraenidae / Anguilliformes), native range (W. Atlantic Bermuda to Brazil + E. Atlantic Cape Verde/Ascension), depth 0-12 m, common length ~40 cm with rare max around 165 cm, preferred temperature 26.1-28.1°C.

additional.name: Snorkel Things care write-up
additional.notes: Cross-checked diet (crustaceans and cephalopods, crab preference), escape risk (tight-fitting hood essential), behavior (active both day and night, relatively shy for a moray).
